query: clinical trial results for baricitinib in alopecia areata

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy and JAK Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Oral ritlecitinib, an inhibitor of JAK3 and the tyrosine kinase expressed in the hepatocellular carcinoma kinase family, is approved by the FDA for patients aged 12 and older with alopecia areata. Similar to baricitinib, continued use is likely necessary to maintain the effects. The initial dose is 50 mg/d. Potential adverse effects are increased risk for severe infection, death, malignancy, lymphoproliferative disorders, major adverse cardiovascular events, thromboembolic events, and hematologic, hepatic, and creatine phosphokinase laboratory abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib is generally considered a safe and well-tolerated medication. However, it increases the risk of severe infections due to its immunosuppressive properties. During clinical trials, the most frequently reported adverse events in alopecia areata patients included upper respiratory tract infections, acne vulgaris, headaches, urinary tract infections, and folliculitis. [5] [18] In addition, there is an increased incidence of herpes zoster infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Alopecia areata: The recommended initial dosage of baricitinib is 2 mg orally once daily. If there is an inadequate treatment response, the dosage may be increased to 4 mg once daily. In cases where patients exhibit complete scalp hair loss, with or without significant eyelash or eyebrow hair loss, treatment with 4 mg of baricitinib once daily, with or without food, should be considered. After achieving an adequate response to 4 mg of baricitinib, the dosage should be reduced to 2 mg once daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Dosage modifications for patients with alopecia areata and renal impairment are as follows: For mild renal impairment (eGFR: 60 to <90 mL/min/1.73 m²), no dosage adjustment of baricitinib is necessary. For moderate renal impairment (eGFR: 30 to <60 mL/min/1.73 m²), the baricitinib dose should be reduced to half. If the patient previously received 4 mg of baricitinib, the dose should be decreased to 2 mg. Similarly, if the patient was once on 2 mg of baricitinib, the dose should be further reduced to 1 mg. For severe renal impairment (eGFR: <30 mL/min/1.73 m²), baricitinib use is not recommended. Pregnancy considerations: Healthcare providers recommend discontinuing baricitinib at least one month before conception. The use of baricitinib during human pregnancy is not well-studied.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy and JAK Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: The choice between topical immunotherapy and JAK inhibitors depends on patient preference and availability. JAK inhibitors are easier to apply but carry the risk of systemic adverse effects. On the other hand, topical immunotherapy requires careful application and may induce discomfort and cutaneous adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: In rheumatoid arthritis, treatment response can be assessed through the Disease Activity Score 28 (DAS28), Clinical Disease Activity Index (CDAI), and the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) response criteria (ACR20, ACR50, and ACR70). For alopecia areata, improvement in the Severity of Alopecia Tool (SALT) score is a valuable indicator for assessing treatment response. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic impairment: For alopecia areata and rheumatoid arthritis, no dosage adjustment is required for mild-to-moderate hepatic impairment. However, its use is not recommended in cases of severe impairment for both conditions. Regarding COVID-19, baricitinib does not require dosage adjustment in mild-to-moderate hepatic impairment. However, in patients with severe hepatic impairment, its usage should be considered cautiously, with a thorough assessment of the potential benefits and risks. [15] Renal impairment: Dosage modifications for renal impairment may vary depending on the condition being treated.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy and JAK Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Following treatment initiation, clinicians typically assess hair growth approximately 3 months later. A recent meta-analysis examining clinical outcomes of contact immunotherapy for alopecia areata indicates that 74.6% of patients with patchy alopecia experience hair regrowth, compared to 54.4% of patients with alopecia totalis and alopecia universalis. Among patients who receive maintenance treatment, the recurrence rate is 38.2%, contrasting with 49% among those who do not receive maintenance treatment. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy and JAK Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with extensive disease, often characterized by more than 50% hair loss on the scalp, may explore treatment alternatives such as topical immunotherapy or oral JAK inhibitors to reduce the necessity of numerous injections associated with intralesional corticosteroids. Moreover, a retrospective study revealed the superior efficacy of topical immunotherapy compared to intralesional corticosteroids in patients with hair loss patches exceeding 50 cm². [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In 2020, following a clinical trial, the FDA also granted emergency use authorization (EUA) for the use of baricitinib in combination with remdesivir to treat COVID-19 in hospitalized patients who need supplemental oxygen, invasive mechanical ventilation, or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). [3] Subsequently, the EUA was revised to authorize baricitinib as a standalone treatment for adult patients. However, for pediatric patients aged 2 to 17, baricitinib continues to be under EUA status. [4] Clinical trials have shown promising results for the use of baricitinib in the treatment of alopecia areata. [2] Following successful outcomes in clinical trials BRAVE-AA1 and BRAVE-AA2, baricitinib has received FDA approval for treating severe alopecia areata in adults. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Totalis -- Treatment / Management -- JAK Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: JAK inhibitors are newer medications that are currently being studied in alopecia areata and totalis. These include tofacitinib, ruxolitinib, and baricitinib. These agents have promising therapeutic benefits due to our current understanding of the pathogenesis of AA. JAK inhibitors have proven efficacious in treating other inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis. There are a few open-label trials for AA patients, which have shown impressive results with significant hair regrowth. However, many patients have a recurrence of hair loss after therapy discontinuation. Side effects of oral JAK inhibitors include viral reactivation, infection, transaminitis, and bone marrow suppression. [12] JAK inhibitors have also been used topically. [18] Topical 2% tofacitinib has been reported to have good results, though relapse upon discontinuation is also high. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy and JAK Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Oral baricitinib, a selective and reversible JAK1 and JAK2 inhibitor, treats alopecia areata by suppressing the activation of T lymphocytes. Patients typically require ongoing therapy to sustain the benefits achieved. The United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has issued a boxed warning for JAK inhibitors, cautioning about the risk of severe infections, mortality, malignancy, major adverse cardiovascular events, and thrombosis. Initial dosing is 2 mg/d with an increase to 4 mg/d if the response is inadequate after 3 months. Patients with severe symptoms may start at the 4 mg/d dose. Upon achieving an adequate response, clinicians reduce the dose to 2 mg. Therapy is discontinued if no improvement is observed after 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The pathogenesis of alopecia areata is attributed to a combination of genetic factors and immune dysregulation. Essential cytokines, such as interferon-γ and interleukin-15, are involved in the disease process, relying on JAKs for intracellular signaling. Baricitinib has demonstrated effectiveness in promoting hair regrowth among adults with severe alopecia areata. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) indicated for rheumatoid arthritis when conventional DMARDs prove inadequate. This medication is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat adult patients with moderately to severely active rheumatoid arthritis who have not responded adequately to other DMARDs, including tumor necrosis factor antagonist therapies. Furthermore, baricitinib is FDA-approved for treating COVID-19, presenting a valuable choice in combination with other therapies for hospitalized patients, including pediatric cases necessitating supplemental oxygen, invasive mechanical ventilation, or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation. This activity comprehensively reviews the indications, contraindications, mechanisms, adverse events, and other crucial aspects of baricitinib therapy within the clinical setting. This activity is tailored to meet the specific needs of an interprofessional healthcare team responsible for treating patients with rheumatoid arthritis, alopecia areata, and COVID-19.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids may promote hair growth, but their adverse effects limit their use. However, patients experiencing rapid and extensive hair loss may find oral glucocorticoids beneficial. Furthermore, relapse occurs within 1 year in approximately one-third of responsive patients, with the likelihood of relapses increasing over time. [1] [25] Ongoing investigational treatments include recombinant IL-2, hydroxychloroquine, and simvastatin with ezetimibe. [21] [22] [23] [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses (Investigational Use)
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical trials have demonstrated the effectiveness of baricitinib and topical corticosteroids in improving the signs and symptoms of severe atopic dermatitis. [6] Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors, such as baricitinib, are also used to treat psoriatic arthritis and vitiligo. [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) comprise a class of high‑potency glucocorticoid agents that are used primarily for their anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. The specific drugs amcinonide, flutikason, budesonid, and fluocinonid are all members of this potent group III corticosteroid category, sharing similar mechanisms of action and therapeutic indications while differing in their individual pharmacokinetic profiles and approved clinical uses. Recognizing that these four agents belong to the same high‑potency corticosteroid group helps clinicians select appropriate topical or systemic therapy, anticipate comparable efficacy, and monitor for class‑related adverse effects such as skin atrophy, hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis suppression, and systemic glucocorticoid exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1339
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, combinations for treatment of acne, includes methylprednisolone, indicating that methylprednisolone—a synthetic glucocorticoid—can be employed as part of combination therapy regimens for managing acne.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids classified as potent (group III) comprise the agents amcinonide, flutikason, budesonide, fluocinolonacetonide and fluocinonide, each of which is a member of this high‑potency corticosteroid group. These drugs share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are commonly employed in dermatologic, respiratory and other inflammatory conditions where a potent corticosteroid effect is required. Their classification together reflects similar pharmacologic strength, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy for patients needing potent corticosteroid treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Fluocinolone acetonide 0.1 mg/mL medicated shampoo (Capex) contains fluocinolonacetonid as its active ingredient, a corticosteroid used to treat inflammatory scalp disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1356
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, combinations for treatment of acne is a therapeutic class that includes methylprednisolon, a corticosteroid often employed in combination regimens for acne management. While methylprednisolon can be effective in this context, it is contraindicated for use in infants (رضيع), highlighting the need for careful patient age assessment when considering this medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1729
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) represent a class of high‑potency corticosteroid agents, and amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid are all members of this group. These four compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, which are commonly employed for their strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in the treatment of dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. Recognizing that amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid belong to the same potent corticosteroid class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy and anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles across these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolon is a systemic glucocorticoid that also appears as an active component in combination corticosteroid formulations used for the treatment of acne. As such, it belongs to the broader class of Glucocorticoids, Systemic, reflecting its widespread anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects throughout the body, and it is included within the category of Corticosteroids, combinations for treatment of acne, indicating its role in dermatologic therapy when paired with other agents. These relationships underscore methylprednisolon’s dual relevance in both systemic glucocorticoid therapy and targeted acne management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2758
retrieved_docs.content: Dermatological Agents [TC] are categorized under the therapeutic class of selenium sulfide, indicating that selenium sulfide serves as the active therapeutic component in these skin‑focused medications, which are employed to treat conditions such as dandruff and seborrheic dermatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1730
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide, flutikason, fluocinolonacetonid, and fluocinonid are all classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroid category. As high‑potency topical corticosteroids, these agents share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are commonly used to treat dermatologic conditions that require potent local therapy. Recognizing that these four drugs belong to the same potency group assists clinicians in selecting appropriate dosing regimens, anticipating similar efficacy, and monitoring for comparable safety concerns such as skin atrophy with prolonged use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1738
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, combinations for treatment of acne and Corticosteroids, weak (group I) are both classified as members of methylprednisolon, indicating that these specific formulations or subgroups fall under the broader methylprednisolon corticosteroid category. This relationship highlights that methylprednisolon encompasses various corticosteroid preparations, including those used in combination therapies for acne and those considered weak (group I) agents, which may influence therapeutic choices and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2761
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, clocortolone, diflorasone, and klobetasol are all classified as antiflogistika, meaning they are anti‑inflammatory agents typically belonging to the corticosteroid family. As antiflogistika, each of these drugs exerts potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects by binding to glucocorticoid receptors, thereby inhibiting the synthesis of inflammatory mediators such as prostaglandins and cytokines. Deflazacort is often used for chronic inflammatory diseases and certain autoimmune conditions, while clocortolone and diflorasone are commonly formulated for topical application to treat dermatologic inflammation. Klobetasol, one of the most potent topical corticosteroids, is employed for severe skin disorders requiring strong anti‑inflammatory action. Recognizing that all four agents share the same therapeutic class (antiflogistika) helps clinicians select the appropriate formulation and potency for specific inflammatory conditions, optimize patient outcomes, and monitor for class‑related adverse effects such as skin atrophy, hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis suppression, and systemic corticosteroid exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Fluocinolonacetonid serves as the active ingredient in fluocinolone acetonide 0.1 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo, and it is likewise the key component of the formulation marketed as fluocinolone acetonide 0.1 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Capex]. This relationship indicates that both the standard medicated shampoo and its Capex‑labeled variant rely on fluocinolonacetonid to provide the therapeutic effects associated with fluocinolone acetonide, a corticosteroid used for treating inflammatory scalp conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines “aplikace / dávkování” (application and dosage) as a mandatory qualifier for a broad spectrum of pharmacologic agents, emphasizing that precise regimens, routes, timing and amounts are essential for safety and efficacy. This qualifier is applied to immunosuppressants (azathioprine sodium, anti‑lymphocyte serum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyclosporine compounds, daklizumab, elipticins, everolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, koformycin, mycophenolic acid, muromonab‑CD3, tacrolimus and takrolimus), to thrombolytic enzymes (anistreplase, streptokinase, tenecteplase, brinase) and to the fibrinolytic protein C (plasmin), all of which dissolve fibrin clots and restore vascular patency. The qualifier also covers numerous oral and topical substances, including the bile‑acid sequestrant colestipol, the proton‑pump inhibitor dexlansoprazol, the 5‑α‑reductase inhibitor finasteride (ATC “other dermatological preparations”, also used for prostate tumors), the antiparasitic ivermectin,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide and fluocinonid are classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroids, a high‑potency subclass of corticosteroid agents. This classification indicates that both amcinonide and fluocinonid share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, including strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that are commonly utilized in dermatologic therapy for inflammatory skin conditions. Recognizing amcinonide and fluocinonid as members of this potent corticosteroid group helps clinicians select appropriate potency levels, balance therapeutic efficacy with safety, and guide appropriate short‑term use to limit systemic exposure.
